result:
The first time I saw the moon through a telescope I cried. Not the delicate, cinematic tear-track kind of crying, but the ugly, hiccupy kind that leaves your face swollen and your ribs sore. The moon wasn’t supposed to look like that—pockmarked, yellowed, hung in the sky like an old photograph left too long in an attic box. I expected silver perfection, a polished coin, something worthy of all the love songs. Instead I got a survivor: impact scars, ancient lava seas, the bruised color of forgetting. I was twelve. My father had dragged the scope onto the frostbitten lawn at 3 a.m. because the Perseids were supposed to peak and he couldn’t sleep again. Mom had been gone three months; the house still carried her in every unopened drawer. Dad tightened knobs, muttered coordinates, his breath a ghost in the torch beam. When he finally stepped back so I could look, the moon rushed at me, so close I felt I might fall upward into it. That was when the crying started. I couldn’t explain that I wasn’t disappointed; I was recognized. The moon and I were both left behind, both still orbiting the empty shape of something we used to reflect.

I kept the crying secret. At school I became the girl who’d touched the moon, a minor celebrity for a week until someone else threw up on the bus and reset the注意力 economy. At home I monitored my father the way amateur astronomers monitor variable stars: judging brightness, guessing at collapse. Some nights he blazed, cooking elaborate stir-fries while singing Springsteen off-key. Other nights he redshifted into his armchair, remote control resting on his chest like a respirator. I learned to measure time in dishes left in the sink, in unopened envelopes, in the gradual migration of his wedding ring from finger to nightstand to the dusty mouth of a coffee mug that said WORLD’S #1 DAD. The moon became my private clock. Each phase felt like a chapter: waxing gibbous, the night I first kissed a boy behind the tennis courts and tasted chlorine and Red Bull; waning crescent, the afternoon Dad forgot to pick me up and I walked three miles through sleet, rehearsing the obscenities I would hurl at him but never did; new moon, the day I found the hospital bracelet in the glove box, his name printed in a font too cheerful for oncology.

By seventeen I could dismantle and reassemble that telescope in the dark, a party trick nobody asked for. I spent my college fund on a used Takahashi with a mount so heavy I herniated two disks hauling it up the apartment stairs. My roommates hated the contraption; it took up half the living room and smelled like damp leaves and metal. But on clear Wednesdays I hosted “Moon Coffee,” blasting NPR’s old theme music, offering burnt espresso to anyone who wanted to look. Philosophy majors asked if the moon existed when nobody observed it; engineering majors corrected my declination; an art student sketched craters on butcher paper and insisted they looked like women screaming. I never mentioned the crying. Instead I said, “The moon is a archive.” They nodded as if that meant something profound. Maybe it did. Every photon that hit my retina had left the lunar surface 1.28 seconds earlier, so technically we were always looking at the past. I liked the lag; it felt merciful. If grief could be delayed like that, buffered, streamed in manageable packets, maybe hearts wouldn’t rupture.

When Dad’s past finally caught up—tumors like tiny new moons orbiting his pancreas—I took a leave from grad school. Moved back into the house that still smelled of my mother’s vanilla diffuser, though the oil had dried years ago. We set up the scope on the same frostbitten lawn, now patched with crabgrass. He was too weak to lift the tripod, so I did the labor while he nested in a lawn chair wrapped with the afghan Mom crocheted when she still believed crafts could hold marriages together. Chemo had stolen his voice, but his eyes worked fine. I aligned the finder, centered the waxing crescent. He looked longer than I expected, one knuckle pressed to his lips as if hushing a child. Finally he whispered, “She always said the moon was a mirror. Shows you what you need, not what you want.” I wanted to tell him I loved him, that I forgave the nights he vanished into static, the grocery-store apologies, the way he kept her sweaters in plastic bins as if bodies could be vacuum-sealed. Instead I said, “It’s 370,000 kilometers away, give or take.” He laughed until the laugh turned into a cough that sprayed the eyepiece with pink stars. I wiped them away with my sleeve, pretending they were merely celestial.

The night after the funeral I drove south until the city lights thinned and the sky reverted to its ancestral darkness. I parked beside a soybean field, set the scope on the hood, pointed it nowhere in particular. The Milky Way dripped like spilled sugar across black marble. No moon. That felt right: a sky with its eye closed. I remembered a lecture about lunar recession—how every year the moon drifts 3.8 centimeters farther from Earth. The same speed fingernails grow. Eventually it will leave entirely, and future generations will surf tides measured in inches, will tell myths about a bright ghost that once haunted the nights. I tried to imagine that loneliness, a planet with no mirror, but my imagination kept collapsing back into the hospital room: the rattle in his chest, the way he squeezed my hand when I told him Perseids were due next week and maybe we could watch. He died during new moon, as if scheduling his exit for maximum darkness. I closed the telescope, left it in the field like a surrender. I didn’t need to see anything anymore; I had seen enough.

Years later I teach eighth-grade science in a town that still turns off streetlights after midnight to save money. My students think the moon is boring—nothing lives there, nothing moves. I assign them the old project: track phases for a month, write what you notice. They groan, predict TikTok withdrawal. One kid, quiet, always drawing skulls on his sneakers, hands in a notebook covered with crater maps. On the last page he has written: “The moon is a scab. Pick it and it bleeds light.” I give him an A and also the Takahashi, which has sat in my closet since the soybean field. His eyes widen like aperture doors. I show him how to balance the mount, how to align the finder. After school we wheel it onto the soccer field. First quarter, crisp sky. He looks, gasps, looks again. His mouth trembles. I wonder if he’s about to cry. Maybe he does; teenage boys have become experts at hiding tears inside hoodie sleeves. Before he leaves he asks why I never look anymore. I tell him the truth: “I’m afraid it will have changed.” He nods as if this makes perfect sense.

Tonight the moon is full, the kind of full that pulls cats onto rooftops and policemen into diners for extra coffee. I stay inside, blinds drawn, grading quizzes about mitosis. But every so often the window glows, a polite knock of photons. I resist until nearly dawn, then slip outside in mismatched socks. The moon hangs low, bruised yellow again, survivor still. I don’t need a telescope; my pupils are plenty wide. I stare until the afterimage burns a silver coin onto the inside of my eyelids. No crying this time—just the recognition that recession is a kind of mercy, that everything leaves eventually at fingernail speed, that mirrors are useless once you accept the shape you’re in. I lift my hand, block the moon entirely, and for a moment the sky is perfectly black, a chalkboard wiped clean. Then I lower my arm and there it is, same as ever: past tense in the present tense, ancient light landing on new skin. Somewhere a teenager is focusing glass, discovering damage worth keeping. Somewhere my father’s atoms are drifting outward, 3.8 centimeters per year, following a path the moon carved long before we thought to miss it.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
